abstract: This theoretical paper examines how generative AI challenges traditional notions of factuality by producing outputs that are plausible but not anchored in empirical evidence. Using fact-checking as an epistemic lens, the authors analyze three established categories of facts—evidence-based, interpretative, and rule-based—and argue they are insufficient for AI-generated content. They propose a new category, 'emergent facts,' drawn from emergence theory and complex systems thinking, to capture the probabilistic, context-dependent, and opaque nature of GenAI outputs, and offer four indicators (accuracy, verifiability, contextual relevance, consistency) for evaluating them.
key_claims: Generative AI outputs cannot be adequately assessed through traditional epistemological frameworks of factuality because they are probabilistic recombinations rather than retrievals of verified information.

Three conventional fact categories used in fact-checking—evidence-based (positivist), interpretative (constructivist), and rule-based (institutional)—each illuminate aspects of factuality but fail when applied to AI-generated content.
A new category, 'emergent facts,' is needed to describe outputs that arise from dynamic interactions between training data, model architecture, and user prompts.
Emergent facts are plausible but unverified, context-dependent, highly variable, and epistemically opaque, and thus require relational assessment rather than binary true/false judgments.
Fact-checking epistemology provides a productive lens for evaluating AI-generated content and supporting AI literacy in computational knowledge environments.
Emergent facts differ from 'algorithmic truth' in that they demand external validation and epistemic scrutiny rather than judgment based purely on coherence or plausibility.
methods: Theoretical and conceptual analysis rather than empirical study.

Literature-based synthesis drawing on philosophy of science (correspondence, coherence, pragmatic theories of truth), sociology of knowledge (Durkheim, Searle, Latour & Woolgar, Foucault), and journalism/fact-checking scholarship.
Comparative epistemological mapping of three fact categories (evidence-based, interpretative, rule-based) against the characteristics of large language models.
Construction of a conceptual framework introducing 'emergent facts' and four evaluative indicators (accuracy, verifiability, contextual relevance, consistency), summarized in Table 1 and Figure 1.
findings: Positivist/evidence-based fact-checking fails for GenAI outputs because they lack ontological referents and traceable provenance.
Constructivist/interpretative approaches highlight how facts are negotiated, but GenAI outputs emerge from opaque probabilistic processes rather than shared social practices.
Institutional/rule-based facts depend on collective agreements and verification protocols that GenAI systems mimic linguistically without actually adhering to.
Emergent facts occupy a distinct epistemic category: computationally constructed, prompt-dependent, variable across models, and not reducible to brute facts.

The four proposed indicators—accuracy, verifiability, contextual relevance, and consistency—offer a structured way to evaluate AI outputs while addressing known limitations like hallucination and bias.
The framework prioritizes evaluation over technical intervention and supports AI literacy and risk mitigation in fact-sensitive contexts.
contributions: Introduces the concept of 'emergent facts' as a novel epistemic category for AI-generated content.
Provides a conceptual framework with four indicators (accuracy, verifiability, contextual relevance, consistency) for evaluating GenAI outputs.
Distinguishes emergent facts from 'algorithmic truth,' clarifying differing epistemic orientations.
Extends fact-checking epistemology from human-centered journalism into computational/AI contexts.
Bridges philosophy of science, AI ethics, and media/information studies by reframing factuality as a dynamic sociotechnical outcome.
Supports AI literacy by offering a structured method to critically assess GenAI limitations.
framing: The paper situates itself at the intersection of epistemology, AI ethics, and media/information studies, engaging debates on truth, objectivity, and verification in journalism while drawing on philosophy of science (positivism, constructivism, institutional facts, emergence theory). It positions fact-checking not just as a journalistic practice but as an epistemological lens for understanding how knowledge is produced and stabilized in AI-mediated sociotechnical systems, contributing to discussions on 'algorithmic truth' and computational epistemology.
